coredata.dc:title: Metal-free carbon-dots nanozyme with oxidase-like activity as photocatalysts for highly efficient detection/reduction of Cr(VI) and antibacterial application
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Huajie
Li, Dai
Zheng, Yutao
Wang, Kui
Zhang, Xiangbo

Yong, Fengyuan
Nie, Jing
Wen, Huiyun
Wu, Jiyong
Xue, Weiming
Huang, Saipeng
coredata.dc:description:
Wastewater containing Cr(VI) and bacteria indicated significant challenges and threats to public health and ecological environments. Hence, it is necessary to construct novel method for detecting and treatment such wastewater. Non-metal-doped carbon dots nanozyme (TA-CDs) were effectively synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method utilizing tartaric acid (TA) and L-tryptophan (L-trp) as ingredients. TA-CDs revealed excellent water solubility, optical stability, and biocompatibility. Particularly noteworthy, TA-CDs (150 μg/mL) exhibited outstanding light-responsive oxidase-like activity, efficiently generating
e
-
,
O
2
∙ -
and
CO
2
∙ -
. Furthermore, TA-CDs illustrated an exceptional linear relationship with Cr(VI) in an extensive detection range and low detection limits (0.51 μM), as well as high selectivity and anti-interference properties. Simultaneously, TA-CDs also demonstrated significant photocatalytic activity, achieving approximately 70.26 % reduction in 40 mg/L Cr(VI) and realized excellent antimicrobial performance (inhibition rates of 97.18 % and 82.67 % against E. coli and S. aureus, respectively). In short, this research not only provided a multifunctional carbon-dots nanoenzyme for recognition, photoreduction of Cr(VI), and photodynamic antibacterial application, but also offered novel strategy and methodology for detection and treatment of water containing heavy metals and microorganisms.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nanozyme
Oxidase-like activity
Photocatalysis
Cr(VI) detection/reduction
Antibacterial activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photocontrolled oxidase activity and steady-state kinetics of TA-CDs
Detection and reduction of Cr(VI) by TA-CDs
Oxidase-like activity and mechanism of TA-CDs nanozyme
Fluorescence detection of Cr(VI) by TA-CDs
Photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) by TA-CDs
Mechanism of detection and photoreduction of Cr(VI)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of TA-CDs. (A) TEM image. Inset: HRTEM image and size distribution; (B) XRD pattern; (C) FT-IR spectrum; (D-G) XPS full spectrum, C1s, N1s, and O1s spectrum; (H) UV–visible absorption, fluorescence spectra. Inset: Images of TA-CDs under sunlight (right) and UV lamp (left); (I) Excitation wavelength dependence.

Oxidase-like activity of TA-CDs. (A) UV–visible absorption spectra under different conditions. Inset: the corresponding images of mixed systems; (B) Influence of pH on catalytic activity; (C) Light-controllable oxidase-like characteristics; (D) Michaelis-Menten equation utilized TMB as substrate under UV LED illumination. Inset: Lineweaver-Burk plot; (E) Effects of different scavengers on TMB oxidation; (F) Light-activated EPR spectra.

Detection and photoreduction of Cr(VI) by TA-CDs: (A) fluorescence spectra of TA-CDs upon mixing of varying Cr(VI) concentrations (0–700 μM); (B) linear connection between F0 − F and Cr(VI) (0–200 μM and 200–700 μM); (C) fluorescence selectivity and anti-interference capability towards various metal ions (700 μM); (D) TA-CDs photoreduction efficiency of Cr(VI); (E) First-order kinetic fitting diagram; (F) Corresponding picture of 40 mg/L Cr(VI) photoreduction under daylight and UV lamp.

The fluorescence quenching and photocatalysis mechanism of TA-CDs (A) UV–Vis spectra of Cr(VI) and TA-CDs fluorescence spectra; (B) UV–Vis spectra of TA-CDs, TA-CDs + Cr(VI), and Cr(VI); (C) Fluorescence lifetimes for TA-CDs and TA-CDs + Cr(VI); (D) XPS spectrum of Cr2p; (E) Impact of various scavengers on the reduction rate of Cr(VI) under UV LED; (F) The mechanism of photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) by TA-CDs.

Photodynamic antibacterial activity of TA-CDs (150 μg/mL). Under different conditions ((I)blank control, (II)UV/4min, (III)TA-CDs, (IV)TA-CDs + UV/2min, and (V)TA-CDs + UV/4min) (A) AGAR plate images of E. coli and S. aureus colonies; (B) E. coli viability; (C) S. aureus viability. (*p＜0.05, **p＜0.01 and ***p＜0.001); (D) SEM images of bacterial; (E) The antimicrobial properties of TA-CDs in real lake water. Inset: the growth plates of agar colonies untreated and treated with (V) TA-CDs + UV/4min.

(A) Preparation of TA-CDs with photoresponsive oxidase-like activity. (B) Schematic illustration of TA-CDs fluorescence detection and photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) under low power ultraviolet light (UV LED). (C) Schematic representation of photodynamic antibacterial activity.

head.ce:title: Metal-free carbon-dots nanozyme with oxidase-like activity as photocatalysts for highly efficient detection/reduction of Cr(VI) and antibacterial application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University, Xi’an, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University, Xi’an, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacy, affiliation: Shandong Second Provincial General Hospital, Jinan, Shandong 250022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Pharmacy, affiliation: Shandong Second Provincial General Hospital

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmacy, affiliation: Shandong Second Provincial General Hospital, Jinan, Shandong 250022, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work and should be considered co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Wastewater containing Cr(VI) and bacteria indicated significant challenges and threats to public health and ecological environments. Hence, it is necessary to construct novel method for detecting and treatment such wastewater. Non-metal-doped carbon dots nanozyme (TA-CDs) were effectively synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method utilizing tartaric acid (TA) and L-tryptophan (L-trp) as ingredients. TA-CDs revealed excellent water solubility, optical stability, and biocompatibility. Particularly noteworthy, TA-CDs (150 μg/mL) exhibited outstanding light-responsive oxidase-like activity, efficiently generating
e
-
,
O
2
∙ -
and
CO
2
∙ -
. Furthermore, TA-CDs illustrated an exceptional linear relationship with Cr(VI) in an extensive detection range and low detection limits (0.51 μM), as well as high selectivity and anti-interference properties. Simultaneously, TA-CDs also demonstrated significant photocatalytic activity, achieving approximately 70.26 % reduction in 40 mg/L Cr(VI) and realized excellent antimicrobial performance (inhibition rates of 97.18 % and 82.67 % against E. coli and S. aureus, respectively). In short, this research not only provided a multifunctional carbon-dots nanoenzyme for recognition, photoreduction of Cr(VI), and photodynamic antibacterial application, but also offered novel strategy and methodology for detection and treatment of water containing heavy metals and microorganisms.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution have become the most serious challenges and threats to human health and ecological systems [1,2]. Among these pollutants, heavy metal ions and bacteria illustrate extremely significant damage, causing a series of physiological and pathological diseases [3,4]. Hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)), a poisonous and poorly biodegradable heavy metal ion [5], which persist, accumulate, and migrate through food chain after entering environment or ecosystem, resulting in severe health hazards such as cancer, respiratory diseases and dermatitis [6–8]. What's worse, microbiological infectious diseases with high morbidity and mortality caused by waterborne viruses and bacteria have become prominent public health problems. Moreover, rampant antibiotic misuse has resulted in continual enhancement of bacterial resistance and ubiquitous presence of pathogenic bacteria like E. coli and S. aureus, potentially triggering outbreaks of waterborne diseases [9,10]. However, the rapid analysis and treatment of wastewater with Cr(VI) and bacteria are usually complicated, costly and high energy-consumption, which is not appropriate for long-term development and widespread application. Consequently, it is essential to create a cost-effective, straightforward, sustainable analytical and purification method for water under diverse circumstances especially in tap water and drinking water systems, which will significantly contribute to addressing public health and environmental concerns.

Nanozymes, as a novel enzyme-like catalytic nanomaterial with catalytic activity similar to natural enzymes, have attracted considerable attention and extensive research interest in the fields of analytical determinations, food safety, green synthesis, new energy resources development, photocatalytic degradation, environmental protection and governance in the past few years [11,12]. Although metal-doped nanozymes demonstrated excellent catalytic activity, their catalytic processes inevitably generated heavy metal pollutants, leading to potential secondary pollution and biotoxicity risks [13]. Among numerous carbon-based nanozymes, carbon dots (CDs) have garnered significant focus because of their remarkable advantages, such as outstanding optical properties, good biocompatibility, excellent photocatalytic performance, and eco-friendliness, positioning carbon- dots nanozymes as promising candidates for multifaceted applications in analytical and photocatalysis [14–16].

However, most current approaches based on carbon dots remain confined to singular applications. For instance, limited in analytical detection, Kwasi et al. [17] synthesized N, S-doped carbon dots by microwave method for accurate identification of Cr(VI) in atmospheric particulate matter samples. Governed by photocatalysis field, Peng et al. [18] constructed a heterojunction photocatalyst for Bi2MoO6/Bi2S3 modified by carbon quantum dots, which derived from waste sludge for effective reduction of Cr(VI). Interestingly, tartaric acid was utilized to enhance the photoreducing activity of Cr(VI) [19,20]. In antibacterial applications, Sun et al. [21] synthesized carbon dots with peroxidase-like activity via a one-pot hydrothermal method, achieving efficient bacterial eradication through photodynamic therapy. Niu et al. [22] synthesized dual-enzyme-mimicking carbon dots, introducing low concentrations of H2O2 for sterilization. Despite high sensitivity, excellent catalytic performance, and potent antibacterial effects of these methods, but low utilization rates and high time consumption. Few research can simultaneously possessed multifunctional application in antibacterial activity, analytical detection and photocatalytic degradation of Cr(VI). Therefore, it is significantly urgent to develop multifunctional carbon dots nanozyme to realize Cr(VI) detection, oxidation conversion and antipathogenic organisms.

In this work, non-metal-doped blue-emissive carbon-dots nanozyme (TA-CDs) was prepared by one-pot hydrothermal process with tartaric acid and tryptophan as precursors. Optical characterization and experimental measurements demonstrated that TA-CDs possessed remarkable fluorescence stability, extremely low cytotoxicity, and especially significant oxidase-like activity. Meanwhile, TA-CDs demonstrated reliable selectivity and anti-interference in Cr(VI) detection with a wide linear range (0–700 μM) and low detection limit (0.51 μM). More importantly, TA-CDs displayed remarkable photocatalysis activity, which efficiently transformed high concentrations of Cr(VI) (40 mg/L) into non-toxic Cr(III) with a high reduction efficiency of 70.26 % (16 min UV LED irradiation). Simultaneously, TA-CDs demonstrated excellent antibacterial properties, restraining the growth of E. coli and S. aureus with strong inhibition rates of 97.18 % and 82.67 %, respectively (4 min of UV LED exposure). Interestingly, the antibacterial rate in practical wastewater sample reached as high as 93.27 %. Additionally, the fluorescence/UV–visible absorption spectra, fluorescence lifetime, electron spin resonance spectra, and photoelectrochemical analysis were explored to elucidate the fluorescence response and catalytic mechanism of TA-CDs. In summary, the multifunctional application platform based on metal-free TA-CDs nanozyme was firstly and successfully constructed for fluorescence detection/photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) and antibacterial application in actual water samples, which was extremely meaningful and practical for the treatment of wastewater containing heavy metals and microorganisms Scheme 1 .

TA (0.1 g, 0.67 mmol) and L-trp (0.1 g, 0.49 mmol) were ultrasonically dispersed in 20 mL deionized water. The blend was moved to a Teflon autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 160 °C for 10 h. Following nature cooling to room temperature, the supernatant was processed via a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The obtained filtrate was continuously stirred and dialyzed (MWCO = 500 Da) in deionized water for 6 h. Finally, TA-CDs was produced by freeze-drying and stored under low-temperature drying conditions for future application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocontrolled oxidase activity and steady-state kinetics of TA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: At room temperature, 100 μL TA-CDs (6 mg/mL) and 100 μL TMB (20 mM) were mixed to 3.8 mL acetate buffer solution (0.2 M, pH = 3.8). Subsequently, after incubation under UV LED (365 nm, 10 W) with a fixed distance (2.0 cm) (Fig. S1) for 1 min, the absorbance at 652 nm was collected. Simultaneously, the optimization of TA-CDs as a mimetic enzyme was achieved by adjusting incubation parameters, such as TA-CDs concentration (25–200 μg/mL), pH range (2.6–5.4), temperature (10–50 ℃) and light exposure time (0–180 s). All previously mentioned experiments were conducted three times to verify the consistency of results.

To investigate the steady-state kinetics of TA-CDs, various concentrations of TMB were incorporated as substrates to TA-CDs buffer solution with optimal oxidase-like activity. Under the same incubation conditions, the enzyme-catalyzed reaction rate was fitted against substrate concentration utilizing the Michaelis-Menten equation (Eq. (1)) to evaluate the relationship between enzyme activity and substrate concentration:
(1)
V
=
V max
×
S
K m
+
[S]

In this equation, V and Vmax meant for the starting and maximum response velocity, respectively. [S] and Km were denoted as the substrate concentration and Michaelis constant, respectively. Specifically, V can be computed from the absorbance of oxTMB at 652 nm based on Lambert-Beer's law. On the other hand, Vmax and Km values can be obtained by plotting the Lineweaver-Burk plot and calculating their corresponding slope and intercept (Eq. (2)):
(2)
1 V
=
K m
V max
×
1 [S]
+
1
V max

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection and reduction of Cr(VI) by TA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence response among TA-CDs and Cr(VI) was evaluated and measured under optimal oxidase-like activity conditions. Initially, 100 μL TA-CDs (6 mg/mL) and 3.8 mL acetate buffer (0.2 M, pH = 3.8) were introduced to a centrifuge tube, succeeded by the addition of 100 μL Cr(VI) with different concentrations to achieve the specified concentration (0–700 μM), which were reacted for 1 min and the fluorescence intensity changes were noted.

The photocatalytic performance of TA-CDs on Cr(VI) was evaluated under UV LED (365 nm, 10 W) with a fixed distance (2.0 cm). The photocatalytic environment was consistent with the detection conditions, and the final concentrations of Cr(VI) were 10, 20, 30 and 40 mg/mL, respectively. To achieve adsorption equilibrium, the mixed solution was placed in the darkness for 10 min. Throughout the subsequent photocatalytic reduction procedure, the absorbance was scanned and recorded at specified time intervals (1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16 min) for real-time detection of Cr(VI) concentration by diphenylcarbazide colorimetric method [23]. Ultimately, the reduction rate (η) of Cr(VI) by TA-CDs was determined utilizing the subsequent equation (Eq. (3)):
(3)
η
=
C
C 0
×
100 %
where C0 denotes the starting Cr(VI) concentration and C indicates the Cr(VI) concentration at a designated time point.

In photodynamic antibacterial experiment, the efficiency of TA-CDs was assessed by spread plate method employing E. coli and S. aureus as models. Initially, all bacteria were cultured in Luria-Bertani (LB) liquid medium for a whole night at 37 °C constant temperature in a shaking incubator. Subsequently, bacterial suspensions were freshly obtained and diluted in LB to approximately 6 × 106 CFU/mL for further application.

Specifically, TA-CDs solutions (0.1 M acetate buffer, pH = 5.6) and bacterial suspension were thoroughly mixed to achieve final TA-CDs concentrations of 150 μg/mL. Next, the mixture was exposed under low power UV LED (365 nm, 10 W) with a fixed distance (5.0 cm) for different durations (0, 2, 4 min). 100 μL treated bacterial suspension (about 3 × 104 CFU/mL) was uniformly dispersed in LB solid medium. Following another 14 h incubation at 37 °C, bacterial viability was evaluated by counting colony-forming units (CFUs). The formula (Eq. (4)) for calculating bacterial survival rate is as follows:
(4)
Bacterial Viability
=
number of CFU (treated)
number of CFU (control)
×
100 %

The influence of TA-CDs on bacterial morphology was examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Initially, the bacterial suspension samples of the blank control and photo-treated group with TA-CDs were centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 5 min to obtain the residue, which was cleaned and centrifuged three times with PBS, followed by fixation in 4 % paraformaldehyde for 12 h. Subsequently, the bacteria were dehydrated stepwise by 10 %, 30 %, 50 %, 70 %, 90 % and 100 % ethanol solutions. Finally, the micromorphological alterations of bacteria were analyzed through SEM after natural drying for 12 h.

All experiments in this research were conducted at least 3 times. Data are presented as mean ± SD and analyzed for statistical significance by SPSS 26.0 software. The significance thresholds were set at *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, and ***p < 0.001, indicating statistically significant differences.

As shown in Fig. S2, quinine sulfate was utilized as a standard [24], the optimal fluorescence quantum yield (QY: 27.11 %) of TA-CDs was achieved through optimization of raw material proportions, reaction time and temperature, which illustrated bright blue fluorescence and laid foundation for Cr(VI) detection with high sensitivity.

To accurately obtain the detailed information of TA-CDs, the micromorphology of TA-CDs was observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). TA-CDs appeared as evenly distributed quasi-spherical nanoparticles, with a diameter of around 3.18 ± 0.39 nm, corresponding to a surface area of around 31.75 nm2(Fig. 1 A). High-resolution TEM image (Inset in Fig. 1A) clearly revealed a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (100) plane of graphite carbon [25]. Additionally, X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns (Fig. 1B) displayed an apparent diffraction peak around 21°, attributed to the (002) plane of graphite carbon [26], which further validated the graphitic structure of TA-CDs.

Thereafter, the surface structure and functional groups of TA-CDs were examined by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). As displayed in Fig. 1C, TA-CDs exhibited a broad peak from 3200 cm−1 to 3500 cm−1, which can be ascribed to stretching vibrational bands of O–H and N–H [27]. This broad peak compared to L-trp may be due to interactions between hydrogen bonds [28]. The distinctive peaks location at 1716 cm−1 and 1624 cm−1 were associated with the asymmetric stretching vibrations of C = O [29]. Notably, the strong vibration band at 1400 cm−1 originated from C-N bonds, confirming the existence of amide bonds [30,31]. Furthermore, the absorption peaks 1602 cm−1 and 1130 cm−1 related to stretching vibrations of C = C and C-O, separately [32]. The infrared spectra further demonstrated the formation of new carbon dot materials with –COOH and –NH2 retained on the surface framework of TA-CDs during the pyrolysis of precursors.

The elementary constitution and chemical state of TA-CDs were ascertained by XPS analysis. XPS spectrum (Fig. 1D) revealed that TA-CDs consisted of C (76.94 %), N (7.82 %), and O (15.24 %) elements, respectively. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 1E) displayed four peaks at 288.80 eV, 286.2 eV, 284.99 eV, and 284.37 eV, representing C = O, C-O, C-N, and C = C/C–C, respectively [33]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 1F) exhibited three constituent peaks, which correspond to pyridine N and pyrrole N amino N (401.58 eV), graphitic N (400.95 eV) and pyrrolic N (399.67 eV) [34]. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 1G) showed three specific peaks at 533.49 eV, 532.12 eV, and 531.11 eV, belonging to –COOH, C-O, and C = O, correspondingly [35]. In summary, the XPS spectrum revealed generous functional moieties on the surface of TA-CDs, in agreement with the FT-IR spectroscopy, which indicated that TA-CDs have excellent hydrophilicity.

The optical characteristics of TA-CDs were assessed and examined through fluorescence (FL) spectroscopy and UV–vis absorption. As illustrated in Fig. 1H, TA-CDs exhibited three typical absorption peaks at around 220 nm, 275 nm and 355 nm, which were attributed to π-π* transitions of C = C in the aromatic sp2 domain, n-π* transitions of C = O/C-N, and defect or surface-state-related transitions involving nitrogen-atoms (N) [30,36]. The TA-CDs solution illustrated brilliant blue fluorescence under UV light and transparent mild yellow under natural light. Furthermore, under excitation wavelength at 375 nm, TA-CDs indicated strong fluorescent emission wavelength at 445 nm. Notably, the excitation wavelength varied between 335 and 395 nm, whereas the emission wavelength of TA-CDs remained constant (Fig. 1I), revealing excitation independence and the uniform surface structure of TA-CDs [37].

Subsequently, the fluorescence stability of TA-CDs was examined under a series of interference conditions, including continuous xenon lamp irradiation for 60 min, exposure to high concentrations of NaCl (0–1 M), adverse temperature conditions (10–50 °C), strong oxidizing H2O2 (0–1 M), varying pH conditions (pH 2–10), and long storage for 30 days under low-temperature, light-avoiding, and dry conditions, the results were displayed by Fig. S3, TA-CDs only exhibited slight fluorescence intensity fluctuations. In conclusion, TA-CDs possessed outstanding optical stability, which exhibited enormous potential for fluorescence detection in complex environmental atmospheres.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oxidase-like activity and mechanism of TA-CDs nanozyme
ce:section.ce:para: By means of a colorimetric assay of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), the oxidase-like activity of TA-CDs was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 2 A, under UV-LED irradiation, the TMB solution turned into visual blue after addition with TA-CDs and exhibited a distinct UV–visible absorption peak at 652 nm. However, visualize color changes were completely unobserved without TA-CDs or UV-LED irradiation, which clearly demonstrated the photoresponsive oxidase-like activity of TA-CDs. Subsequently, the interference factors were considered to investigate and evaluate the oxidase-like activity, exemplified by pH, illumination time, TA-CDs concentration and temperature. As depicted in Fig. S4A, the optimal catalytic performance was observed at 150 μg/mL of TA-CDs. Meanwhile, the absorbance of ox-TMB at 652 nm reached its maximum value at a pH of 3.8 and temperature of 30 °C, indicating the highest oxidase-like activity of TA-CDs (Fig. 2B, Fig. S4B). Furthermore, the absorbance illustrated a stepwise ascent under continuous on–off cyclic illumination, highlighting the photoactivated oxidase-like characteristics of TA-CDs (Fig. 2C).

Notably, the steady-state kinetics of TA-CDs oxidase-like activity followed the typical Michaelis-Menten model. By plotting the Lineweaver-Burk curve utilizing Michaelis-Menten equation [38], Vmax and Km values were 92.94 × 10−8 M·s−1 and 0.46 mM, respectively (Fig. 2D). Compared to reported oxidase-like nanozyme (Table S1), TA-CDs exhibited superior Vmax and lower Km at lower radiant energy light sources, demonstrating excellent affinity and remarkable enzyme catalytic performance.

To explore and validate the oxidase-like activity mechanism of TA-CDs, IPA, Trp, EDTA•2Na, BQ, and CuCl2 as scavengers were utilized to remove •OH, 1O2, photogenerated hole (h+),
O
2
∙ -
, and e-, respectively [27,39]. As shown in Fig. 2E, oxidase-like activity of TA-CDs was significantly inhibited only after the addition of BQ and CuCl2, while other scavengers illustrated minimal impact on the oxidation activity of TMB. Subsequently, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy was conducted utilizing DMPO as a
O
2
∙ -
scavenger to further confirm the generation of
O
2
∙ -
. As described in Fig. 2F, the EPR spectrum of TA-CDs/DMPO didn't exhibit any characteristic peaks in a dark environment (blue line), whereas the typical signal peaks appeared after introduction of specific light illumination, demonstrating the presence of
O
2
∙ -
[40]. In summary, TMB was oxidated by producing electrons and
O
2
∙ -
generated by light-activated TA-CDs, which provided a solid foundation for subsequent application in photoreduction Cr(VI) and antimicrobial applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Cr(VI) by TA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: For the specific recognition of Cr(VI) by TA-CDs, the response time was comprehensively considered and investigated. Firstly, TA-CDs exhibited optimal oxidase-like activity, facilitating the subsequent photoreduction of Cr(VI). Additionally, the TA-CDs system's fluorescence intensity immediately decreased and remained stable within 10 min after addition of Cr(VI), indicating short and quick response time (Fig. S5). Therefore, the optimum detection time of Cr(VI) was 1 min in the acetate buffer (0.2 M, pH = 3.8).

As exhibited in Fig. 3 A, with the increased Cr(VI) concentration (0–700 μM), the fluorescence intensity of TA-CDs at 445 nm steadily declined. Simultaneously, a significant quenching of blue fluorescence was observed under UV light, which aligned with the trend of fluorescence spectra changes (inset of Fig. 3A). Hence, the linear relationship between Cr(VI) concentration and fluorescence intensity could be established and fitted. According to the results in Fig. 3B, TA-CDs illustrated excellent linear relationship in low and high concentration range, respectively. F0 − F established an excellent linear relationship with low concentration ranges of 0–200 μM, the linear regression equations was F0 − F = 24.53 [Cr(VI)] + 21.14 (R2 = 0.998). The calculated limit of detection (LOD) was 0.51 μM utilizing 3δ/k (S/N = 3). Meanwhile, within the high concentration range of 200–700 μM, the linear regression equation was F0 − F = 11.51 [Cr(VI)] + 2921.18 (R2 = 0.997). F and F0 denoted the fluorescence intensity of TA-CDs with and without Cr(VI), respectively. Compared to current reports on Cr(VI) detection, TA-CDs exhibited higher sensitivity and a wider linear range (Table S2).

Moreover, selectivity and anti-interference also was important indicators for fluorescence sensors. The effects of common metal ions (K+, Na+, Ag+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Gd2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Cr3+, Fe3+) on the fluorescence intensity of TA-CDs were evaluated. As illustrated in Fig. 3C, the relative fluorescence intensity was markedly decreased by merely Cr(VI), which was consistent with the fluorescence visual quenching phenomenon (inset of Fig. 3C).

Meanwhile, tap and drinking water were utilized as actual samples for evaluating the measurement accuracy and dependability of TA-CDs in complicated samples. TA-CDs also exhibited satisfactory recovery rates and small errors in real water samples (Table S3), the recoveries of tap and drinking water with different concentrations of Cr(VI) were 98.20 %−104.87 %, RSD ≤ 4.47 % and 97.32 %−101.61 %, RSD ≤ 3.10 %, respectively. Therefore, based on the fluorescence probe of TA-CDs, the detection of Cr(VI) not only exhibited excellent selectivity and anti-interference capability, but also provided an efficient and reliable means for identifying Cr(VI) in complex environments, which greatly improved the accuracy and reliability in complicated samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) by TA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: After the effective identification of Cr(VI) by TA-CDs, the post-processing of Cr(VI) was essential for health protection. Under UV LED illumination, the reduction effect of TA-CDs solution toward Cr(VI) was quantitatively evaluated. As shown in Fig. 3D, the starting concentration of Cr(VI) and illumination time are important influencing factors on photocatalytic reduction rate. It is notable that when the starting Cr(VI) concentration is 10 mg/L and 40 mg/L, the reduction rates of Cr(VI) reach 96.38 % and 70.26 % within 16 min, demonstrating the outstanding photocatalytic activity of TA-CDs. Subsequently, the pseudo-first-order kinetic model was fitted by the photocatalytic reduction data of Cr(VI) for various starting Cr(VI) concentrations (10, 20, 30, and 40 mg/L), the k values were 0.2238, 0.1788, 0.1239, and 0.078 min−1, respectively (Fig. 3E). As the Cr (VI) concentration increased, the photocatalyst reduction velocity diminished, indicating that the active sites of the photocatalyst were saturated at elevated Cr(VI) concentrations [41]. To further understand the extent of photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI), the XPS spectrum of Cr2p was utilized to analyze the photocatalytic system. As expected, the reduction ratio reached 75.31 % (Fig. S6) at the concentration of 40 mg/L, reflecting the efficient conversion from Cr(VI) to Cr(III), which matched the visual changes in daylight and UV light (Fig. 3F).

Additionally, the effect of Cr(VI) photocatalytic reduction was also evaluated in tap water and drinking water samples. TA-CDs exhibited efficient reduction capabilities even at high concentrations (40 mg/L) of Cr(VI) samples, which fully demonstrated the practicability and effectiveness of TA-CDs (Fig. S7). In conclusion, this integrated detection and reduction method can real-time monitor Cr(VI) concentration changes and promptly reduce environmental pollution, which is of great significance for environmental conservation, human well-being and technological innovation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of detection and photoreduction of Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: The common quenching mechanism of TA-CDs was analyzed by luminescence, UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence lifetime [42]. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the characteristic absorption peak of Cr(VI) at around 350 nm can be observed from the UV–Vis absorption spectra (red curve), which largely overlapped with the fluorescence excitation spectra of TA-CDs (purple curve), suggesting that the response between TA-CDs and Cr(VI) may be attributed to Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE) mechanism [43]. Furthermore, the UV absorption peak of TA-CDs remained unchanged after the introduction of Cr(VI) (Fig. 4B), indicating that new substances didn't generated during the quenching process and can't be attributed to the static quenching (SQ) mechanism [44]. To deeply understand the response mechanism, the fluorescence decay curves of TA-CDs and TA-CDs + Cr(VI) (Fig. 4C) were nearly identical and the fluorescence lifetimes were13.64 and 13.31 ns, respectively, which implied that there is no significant energy transfer between TA-CDs and Cr(VI), and dynamic quenching (DQ) or FRET can be essentially neglected [45]. Furthermore, the generation of Cr(III) was observed from XPS spectrum of TA-CDs after the introduction of Cr(VI) (Fig. 4D), which suggested that the quenching reaction may undergo electron transfer (ET). Therefore, the fluorescence quenching between TA-CDs and Cr(VI) may be mainly induced by IFE and ET [46].

The electron transfer mechanism is a crucial and mainstream theory in elucidating the process of photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI). Firstly, the photoelectric properties of TA-CDs was evaluated through UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV-DRS), valence band XPS, photocurrent response and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis. As displayed in Fig. S8A-B, the bandgap (Eg), valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) energy of TA-CDs are 4.06 eV, 3.02 eV and −1.04 eV (ECB = EVB − Eg), respectively, which was more negative than the standard reduction potential (
O 2
/
O
2
∙ -
, −0.33 eV), indicating that photo-generated electrons can effectively transform
O 2
to
O
2
∙ -
[47,48]. Furthermore, in transient photocurrent response and EIS measurement (Fig. S9C–D), TA-CDs exhibited a high photocurrent response signal and low electrochemical impedance values, indicating efficient separation and transfer of photogenerated charge carriers [49].

Moreover, to verify the active substances of photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI), radical scavengers were introduced. Benzoquinone (BQ), isopropanol (IPA), KBrO3, EDTA-2Na, and crystal violet (MV) were utilized as scavengers for
O
2
∙ -
, •OH and
e
-
, h+, and
CO
2
∙ -
, accordingly [50]. As exhibited in Fig. 4E, when EDTA-2Na and IPA were incorporated, the h + and •OH in the reaction solution were scavenged, but the reduction rate of Cr(VI) only changed slightly, which were 71.66 % and 69.12 % respectively. In contrast, the degree of photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction after the introduction of KBrO3 and BQ decreased to 23.20 % and 58.68 %, respectively, indicating that
e
-
and
O
2
∙ -
are essential to the procedure of reducing Cr(VI), which was unanimous with the prior discussion on oxidase-like activity and band structure analysis. It is noteworthy that MV also successfully demonstrated the involvement of
CO
2
∙ -
in the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) [51].

In conclusion, Fig. 4F and Text S3.1 revealed a possible mechanism of TA-CDs photocatalytic process. Through characterization of photoelectric properties and radical scavenging experiments, it was hypothesized that TA-CDs generated
e
-
and h+ under UV LED irradiation, where
e
-
interacts with
O 2
to form
O
2
∙ -
[52]. Subsequently, through the interaction of
e
-
and
O
2
∙ -
, seriously poisonous Cr(VI) was successfully converted to safer form Cr(III). Moreover, due to the functionalization of TA-CDs with TA precursors, which can prevent the recombination of electron-hole pairs, boost electron transfer within the system, and generate highly reducing
CO
2
∙ -
through photoexcitation and h+ capture, further enhancing the efficiency of photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction [53,54].

There is no doubt that reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as
O
2
∙ -
, •OH, 1O2 and H2O2, possess potent oxidative properties that can induce oxidative destruction of bacterial organelles or directly eliminate bacteria, leading to speedy antibactericidal effects [55,56]. Previous research has demonstrated that TA-CDs exhibited oxidase-like activity and generated extensive ROS (
O
2
∙ -
), thus offering significant advantages as antimicrobial materials. Firstly, the cytotoxicity of TA-CDs was assessed through CCK-8 experiments. The results demonstrated that the cell viability remained around 97.33 % even at a concentration of 200 μg/mL (Fig. S9), indicating environmentally friendly and excellent biocompatible properties of TA-CDs [57].

Furthermore, the photocatalytic antimicrobial impact of TA-CDs was researched by plate counting way. The experimental result revealed that TA-CDs (150 μg/mL) or a 4 min low power UV LED didn't exhibit significant antibacterial effects against these two bacteria, respectively. However, when TA-CDs and the low-power light source were combined and simultaneously possessed, the viability of E. coli and S. aureus decreased significantly to 97.18 % and 82.67 %, respectively (Fig. 5 A–C). To further evaluate the antibacterial properties of TA-CDs, the microstructural alterations of E. coli and S. aureus under various circumstances were observed via SEM. Notably, compared with the blank control, the bacterial illustrated obvious collapse and deformation of cell morphology, as well as leakage and accumulation of intracellular materials under the photocatalysis of TA-CDs (Fig. 5D). To investigate the antibacterial properties of TA-CDs in real wastewater samples, the water of Yulan Lake (Northwest University, China) was selected and collected as a model of wastewater for antimicrobial experiment. As exhibited in the Fig. 5E, the untreated wastewater exhibited prominent growth of bacterial colonies with various shapes and colors on the agar plates, while the wastewater treated with TA-CDs displayed a significant antibacterial effect, with an inhibition rate of more than 93.27 %. These results demonstrated the excellent antibacterial properties of TA-CDs in real wastewater samples, suggesting that this research may provide an effective and feasible strategy for wastewater treatment, which was extremely meaningful in the domains of food security, environmental management and public health.

In summary, the metal-free TA-CDs was successfully synthesized by tartaric acid and L-tryptophan, which was verified through optical characterization techniques, including TEM, XRD, XPS, and FT-IR. Notably, TA-CDs exhibited significant oxidase-like activity under low power UV LED, producing abundant
e
-
,
O
2
∙ -
and
CO
2
∙ -
. Based on the optical characteristics, the multifunctional platform integrating Cr(VI) recognition, photoreduction, and photodynamic antibacterial capabilities were firstly explored and constructed. The platform demonstrated selective and quantitative identification of Cr(VI) with a low LOD of 0.51 μM (linear range: 0–200 μM and 0–700 μM). Meanwhile, TA-CDs (150 μg/mL) also illustrated excellent photocatalysis activity, which achieved approximately 70.26 % reduction in 40 mg/L Cr(VI) (16 min UV LED irradiation). Additionally, TA-CDs displayed robust antibacterial properties versus E. coli and S. aureus under photodynamic conditions, achieving 97.18 % and 82.67 % antibacterial rate, respectively (4 min of UV LED exposure). Notably, the antibacterial rate in the wastewater sample also reached 93.27 %. In brief, the multifunctional nanozyme sensing platform not only provided a reliable and promising approach for detecting, preventing and treating wastewater containing heavy metals and microorganisms, but also it was extremely meaningful and valuable in food security, environmental regulation and human health surveillance.

Huajie Chen: Conceptualization, Investigation, Validation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Dai Li: Validation, Visualization. Yutao Zheng: Methodology. Kui Wang: Validation. Xiangbo Zhang: Resources. Sheng Zhou: Investigation. Shansi Wei: Conceptualization. Fengyuan Yong: Data curation. Nie Jing: Methodology. Huiyun Wen: Validation. Jiyong Wu: Validation, Supervision. Weiming Xue: Formal analysis, Data curation. Saipeng Huang: Validation, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2017 M613190) and Natural Science Basic Research Program of Shaanxi Province (2019JQ-461).

ce:section.ce:para:
